era_context: The early Islamic period following the Prophet Muhammad's death in 632 CE. The Rashidun Caliphate under Umar ibn al-Khattab (r. 634–644) is in its expansionist phase, conquering the Levant, Egypt, and Mesopotamia from Byzantine and Sassanid control. The Byzantine Empire under Heraclius I is in decline after decades of war with Persia. China is in the early Tang Dynasty (618–907), a period of cultural and administrative flourishing under Emperor Taizong.

events.title: Conquest of Alexandria and Egypt by Rashidun forces
events.description: The final phase of the Islamic conquest of Egypt culminated in the fall of Alexandria to Rashidun Caliphate forces under Amr ibn al-As. This completed the conquest begun in 639 and marked the end of Byzantine control of Egypt after nearly 350 years. The conquest was facilitated by internal Byzantine religious divisions (Coptic-Chalcedonian disputes) and war fatigue. Egypt's grain resources became crucial to the Islamic state's economy and power.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
John of Nikiu, Chronicle (Coptic source, 7th century)

events.certainty_note: The fall of Alexandria in 641 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. Dating to 640 reflects the final stages of the siege; sources vary slightly on exact year.

events.title: Battle of Firaz (or Marj al-Rahit)
events.description: A major engagement between Rashidun forces and a Byzantine-Ghassanid coalition, fought near the Euphrates. The Rashidun victory under Umayyad commanders consolidated Islamic control of the Levant and demonstrated the military superiority of the early Islamic armies. This battle is sometimes dated to 633 or 636; sources disagree on exact chronology.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The battle's date is disputed among modern scholars. Some place it in 633, others in 636 or 640. The event itself is confirmed, but exact dating remains uncertain.

events.title: Emperor Taizong's consolidation of Tang administrative reforms
events.description: Emperor Taizong (r. 626–649) continued implementing the equal-field system (均田制, juntian zhi) and civil service examination reforms that strengthened centralized bureaucratic control. These reforms reduced aristocratic power and created a meritocratic administrative class. By 640, the system was well-established, contributing to Tang stability and cultural flourishing.

sources.name: Book of Tang (Tang Shu), compiled by official historians
Records of administrative edicts and land surveys

events.certainty_note: Tang administrative reforms are well-documented in official histories and archaeological evidence of land records.

events.title: Harsha's death and fragmentation of his empire
events.description: King Harsha of Kannauj, who had ruled much of northern India and patronized Buddhism, died around 647 CE (some sources place death in 640). His death marked the end of the last major unified Hindu-Buddhist empire in northern India before Islamic sultanates. His empire fragmented into regional kingdoms, and political power shifted southward to the Deccan.
key_figures: Xuanzang (Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited Harsha's court)

sources.name: Xuanzang, Records of the Western Regions (大唐西域記)

events.certainty_note: Harsha's death date is traditionally given as 647 CE, but some sources suggest 640. The exact year remains debated among scholars.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Firaz remains contested; some modern scholars argue it occurred in 633 or 636 rather than 640, or that it may conflate multiple engagements. Harsha's death date is similarly uncertain—sources range from 640 to 647 CE. The exact chronology of the Alexandria siege (639–641) varies in sources; some place the final fall in 641 rather than 640.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 640 CE is moderate but regionally uneven. Islamic sources (al-Tabari, Ibn al-Athir) are rich but compiled centuries later and reflect Abbasid-era perspectives. Byzantine sources are sparse for this period. Chinese sources are abundant and relatively contemporary. Indian sources are primarily literary and epigraphic, with dating uncertainties. The conquest narratives in Islamic sources may emphasize religious motivation over political and economic factors. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: Conquest of Alexandria led to full Islamic control of Egypt
Taizong's reforms continued under his successors
Harsha's death fragmented northern India, enabling later Islamic sultanates
